speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us when you were taken away and arrested for the last time.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was you know, after Jewish uprising. One year, about, later was Warsaw uprising. I mean, the whole town was rising against the German. And it was done 1st June, July-- 1st of July. 1St, August. 1st August It's hard for me to pick up the German-- the English, in other words. And start uprising. And we was located in north part of the Warsaw, called Starowka-- Old Town. And German try divided all Warsaw pieces. And one of the piece what German divide was Starowka-- mean the Old Town. And we was fighting there 30 days. And end of the month, we, after 30 days, we was without ammunition, without-- the whole time there was not enough ammunition and guns. And all fighting people has only these small guns and small rifles. Nothing heavy. And German has the airplane, and also artillery-- heavy artillery. That was the reason it was not successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do you mean when you say we? Were you part of an organized group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I was in the-- almost 90% Polish young people was involved in underground movement, one or other way. There was only one problem. After the Warsaw uprising start, was not enough ammunition, guns, and et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the same problem what was in the Jewish ghetto. We was-- it was stick against the tanks. Many young people, you know, risk life and go to the-- close to the tank and throw the benzene. I mean the-- not kerosene-- how did they? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What you put in the tank. In the car. Dynamite?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the tank. To running the car. Gasoline? Gasoline. One [INAUDIBLE] bottled gasoline, and throw to the tank. And you know, that was one way what we later there took from German who go away from the tank the ammunition or the guns. That was the way to fight. And sometimes the young fellow was killed. And many people was, this time, killed. 200,000 people was almost destroyed in Warsaw. That was one of the biggest, you know, battle, civilian battle. I can say so. And what happened-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] end of the 30 days, we supposed to surrender. And some part of people with the guns go through the canal to the other part of the town and fight again. We was-- I was very close to my family, the house, and was without ammunition. And I meet my parents and daughters-- sisters. And it was maybe six hour after I meet my family, the German go to a basement and ask everybody out-- [GERMAN] And we go to buy food to outskirt the Warsaw. Later, to the [NON-ENGLISH]. That was like depot of the-- depot of the trains. A big car. And there was selection. Old people on one side, young people on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, young, why? Because young was fighting. And my mother and father go on one side-- supposed to go on one side. And my parents would not separate from us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meantime, they a couple hours before, I prepare some agreement with the German who was an Austrian fellow who was working for-- for the physician who selected the people. And I give her each person from my family-- $20, gold. And what happened-- we supposed to, after a couple hours, go away. I mean, not go to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? In the meantime, a Red Cross from Switzerland arrived to the camp, and SS not like show the people there, so many. Throw us, everybody to the car. And this agreement-- what I make, with this German-- Austrian guy, disappear. I lose money, and I lose possibility go away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we was put by force to the car-- cattle car. Close the door. And we go a couple of days around looking for place who, in Germany-- camp what like take us. Many camps was not interested. There's not room. We not take this. And after all, we arrived to Weiden, Germany. Later, there to Floss. And that was in Floss was the place-- Flossenburg was the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the conditions of the cattle cars that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, cattle cars was terrible, like the German feel we are not human being. And it was maybe 50, 60 people. And, you know, was not really room for laying-- I mean, sleeping. One was standing, one was sleeping. And you know, no toilet, nothing. That was terrible condition. It was very, very bad. And this time, I was sick. And I was thinking I dying. And after we arrived to Floss, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I ask the man who was on the locomotive, give me little bit hot water. Because my stomach was very-- I was thinking, one today and I die. And the fellow give me the water, and also give me the steam from-- and I drink this. And my mother was very, very upset and was scared. And-- because at this time, I was with my mother, father, and our cook, and also two sister, who I go with this family. Because I was impressed. I was feeling, we do the same what my mother and father did 1918 or '17, I don't remember exactly. And I be working with-- I heard these people, my family-- if we be together in some place where we be work together and help each other. And happened, we go to concentration camp. And my mother was prepared with this good experience during World War first. Give everybody from us, the gold. And we put everything what we had there, my mother and father [INAUDIBLE]. We split. And my mother prepare. For example, for me, I remember it was attache cases. And where is the metal lock, put there gold coins. Special the Russian rubles, gold rubles-- small one. And after we arrived to concentration camp, we lose everything there, because it was taken from us. And we go to bathroom, take shower. And later there, we receive new clothes. I mean, new clothes-- these clothes what other people use, only it was disinfected. And our gold to-- search for gold for [INAUDIBLE], for some other stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you use for food during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: During transport, you mean? It was-- we have with us a little bit-- my mother prepare and we took. And we was hungry. Nobody give us nothing. I not remember we have some. Really I don't-- I know this was-- I could not eat anyway, because I was sick. And nobody gave the food. I not remember we receive something.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Flossenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was nine months in Flossenburg. And it was enough. 23rd May, we was liberated by American Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Flossenburg like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flossenburg is in Germany, very close to Czech border, very close to the [INAUDIBLE]. And our mountain. And during the summer, you not see the fly, because it was too cold, no good for fly. And for us was not the best, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? This camp was selected for only politician and bandit. Why bandit? For example, my neighbor in the same room was fellow who kill own mother. Because two brothers played soccer and mother was so upset so many times-- don't play so [? evening ?] and so-and-so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kill, cut the ball by-- by a knife. And this young fellow-- German fellow-- German fellow, before the 1930s-- something like this-- during night, kill mother, and was sent there to this camp, for example. That's kind of people was there. Or politician, like General Canaris. If you remember, there was one of brilliant head in German Army, in the [GERMAN] [INAUDIBLE]. Because it was against Hitler, he was sent there. And a couple of days before American was there, was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage in Flossenburg with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pure luck. My sister-- we arrived to the Flossenburg, and the girls were sent to block number 17 or 18. And my-- my sister was very sick. And some-- no, excuse me-- was sick, not very sick. And some unit, and but medical unit from the camp give some medicine, this [NON-ENGLISH]. And one guy named Yanitski from Poznan, young fellow treat [INAUDIBLE] my sister something. And from this moment, my sister, my mother said, listen, we have family there-- is father and son. And give us our name. And after day-- or the same day-- this fellow look for us, and give our suggestion what we supposed to do. Very important thing in my life. This fellow stop and said, we was not searched yet. Meaning, we own everything in our pockets. And I own a gold watch-- very valuable and very famous, because after I finish high school, I receive from my father. And this fellow said, stop, look for us, and said, don't give these people-- don't take everything with you to pass along. Give me-- later, then I give you back. And we know this guy, because he has some message from my sister. We risk. And what we can, we give him back. Meaning, give him and feeling maybe the fellow will give us back. I give watch, I give some gold. Some, I keep. And after, we go through the bathroom, or through the bath, and we receive new-- new other dress, we meet this guy together. And this guy give me back watch, what I not expect. And give me the other stuff. I said, listen, watch, I take, because for me, it's memory. The other, you keep. And this moment-- from this moment, start good relationship. And this guy give-- has good reputation there, and was on the position-- can do something for me. Working for hospital. Prepare for me the job in [NON-ENGLISH], mean in a [NON-ENGLISH]. Where you make, you know, wood, carpentry shop. And it was very good, because one of the best was on the top barrack. Because the barracks was on the side of the mountain. And was 150 steps. And the barrack was at the top. That was good, because German not like go there so often. And I was working there. And listen again-- I was working there maybe two weeks. And one day, from transport, people who came from Hungary, was bring to our carpentry shop couple boxes. Like years ago, poor people has not suitcases like we have-- it was wood suitcases. And also something what has to do with the wood. And you know German-- never throw out what is useful. And the old wood what was there, and plywood, was sent to our shop. And what happened-- I was there the youngest there. in the-- There was a couple Russian officers, because it was [INAUDIBLE] officers. And German-- German people who were there. And I was the guy who is supposed to cook in the can, like this, potato, and to watch through the window if SS man not going in. And we was alone, nobody watch us. And I was doing this. And one day, I-- because the can was open-- the German can is something like this. And it was open and the steam going out. And I pick up, one day, something what-- I don't know. You remember, years ago, was if you erase, or something, you need mirror.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the back of the mirror is the support so it can stay like this, right? And a good handle, and very heavy plywood. I said, oh, that's fine. I put over this. And the steam not going out and quicker we are ready, [? mean ?] the potato.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, I saw the steam going out. I go there and I pick up the cover with the metal thing. And in this moment, everything split up. The plywood split up. And around this, maybe 10, maybe 15 pieces 20 dollar gold go on the floor. And these people who are working there-- everybody jumped there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was crazy. I was-- what do I do? I pick up a couple of pieces, too. And this was a reason, later, that we can change for bread with people who work for SS cantina, and has all time left over a little bit bread. That help us for a couple of months-- you know, change the dollars for a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [INAUDIBLE] for me. And I don't think if I can say lucky. I don't know I can say so or not. I go through the appellplatz, big platz, where mostly it was 1,000 people going back and forth. And I look. And something was bright on the floor. Small thing, like shiny. I pick up, and I pick up diamond. Like this. [INAUDIBLE] somebody there. [LAUGHS] And thousand people daily, going back and forth never see this. I pick up this. That was truly so we can survive-- that pure luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with the diamond?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diamond, I bring later to-- to mother. And, you know, the gold coin we trade for food. And, you know, that's-- my feeling is, if I can help somebody, I can do this, because Dr. Yanitski, now, the fellow who helped me there, is doctor. Dr. Yanitski was the fellow who helped me very much. And thank God. And this guy, I stayed in life, and my father, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in the hospital in Flossenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was working-- again, after I finish-- after was open place for one guy in hospital. And Dr. Yanitski, I mean, before he was a doctor, Yanitski was working there. He said, Paul, come on. I have for you, job. That was the best job. Meantime, Yanitski prepare for my father a place what was in dentist place, working for dentist. And prepare dentist-- cook, eat, and so on. That's French guy. And that was the reason my father was survived, too. And I was working in hospital. And it was-- thank God, it was enough-- almost enough soup-- not bread-- soup. That's-- and never-- I never was on the outside, where it was temperature terrible, and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hospital, I was-- it was up to the people who was managed the hospital. I was working sometimes-- for example, once, I was sent there hold the amputation and [INAUDIBLE] after-- after the fellow from the east front was sent to us with frozen leg. For example, that was something. And later-- and all the dirty job, I was doing in my cell. After operation was done, the patient was sent to my cell, where I was working. Couple other guys. And we do everything. Cleaning people, change the bandage or-- or something like this. Put the bed or something-- that was my job. Good job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people that were the patients there? Any patient who has something special. For example, there was many people who has some extra-- they look different, or has something not usual. Doctor-- our doctor who was later there in Nuremberg [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for [INAUDIBLE] for crime or something like this. And try see what happened. For example, somebody has big [NON-ENGLISH], how you call? This stuff here. [? Goiter. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how you call it. Cut and see what is, why? And so on and so on. You know, that's like animal. Here, in the United States, we take [? surge ?] for a small mouse or small animal. There, was the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you deal with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, for me, it was very hard. Because before the war, in Warsaw, one near our house, the car kicked somebody, and it was blood. I go all over the other [? blocks. ?] And I not like see the blood. In concentration camp, was only blood. You know how I feel. And it was way-- be there or be dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very hard for me. That's a reason I not go to medical school after the concentration camp. I finish polytechnic institute. I not going to medical way. What kept you alive in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, first I was young and I was thinking all the time what must be done. What God [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] has for me-- that happened. And besides this, I like survive, because I was thinking we have something-- was family together. You know, also probably only what I feel-- my feeling I must survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see many Jews in Flossenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have a photo. My next neighbor was doctor-- right now, he's doctor. It was 10 years younger, like me. And leaving in New York. And I have the name and address. And many was-- not many-- only, you don't know who was Jewish. It was not with the stripe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll pick up on the next tape.
